threats.title: Isolate Liquidation NFT Theft: Anyone Can Claim Auctioned NFT Instead of Highest Bidder
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeIsolateLiquidate, the NFT collateral is transferred to params.msgSender (the caller of isolateLiquidate) instead of loanData.lastBidder (the highest bidder who escrowed funds). The highest bidder's escrowed funds are consumed to repay the debt, but a third-party caller receives the NFT. Any user can call isolateLiquidate after the auction ends and steal the NFT that the highest bidder paid for.
threats.investigation_rationale: The auction escrow pattern implies the highest bidder should receive the NFT. The code transfers to msgSender without verifying that msgSender == lastBidder. This creates a direct asset theft vector where the attacker pays at most the extraBorrowAmounts (difference between bid and current debt), while the bidder loses their entire escrowed bid amount.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc721TransferIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate(nftAssetData, params.msgSender, params.nftTokenIds) and erc721TransferOutLiquidity(nftAssetData, params.msgSender, params.nftTokenIds) use params.msgSender instead of loanData.lastBidder

preconditions: An isolate auction exists with loanStatus == LOAN_STATUS_AUCTION
Auction end timestamp has passed (block.timestamp > auctionEndTimestamp)
A last bidder has escrowed funds in totalBidAmout

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: IsolateLogic.sol executeIsolateLiquidate, lines near end of function
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: NFT transfer recipient is params.msgSender not loanData.lastBidder
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: erc721TransferIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate and erc721TransferOutLiquidity both use params.msgSender as destination. Bidder's escrowed funds (erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity) pay the debt. No check that msgSender == lastBidder.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No access control restricting caller to last bidder. No validation require(params.msgSender == loanData.lastBidder)

analysis_questions: Is there any off-chain or on-chain mechanism that restricts who can call isolateLiquidate?
Can an attacker reliably call this before the actual bidder on the target chain?
What is the maximum profit from bidder escrow theft?
validation_requirements: Confirm no hidden access control in the dispatch/proxy layer
Build PoC: deploy auction, bid, end auction, have third party call liquidate
Verify attacker receives NFT and bidder's funds are consumed

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Bidder bids 10 ETH on underwater NFT worth 12 ETH. 2) Auction ends. 3) Attacker calls isolateLiquidate. If borrow=9.5 ETH, attacker pays 0 extra (bid covers debt). Bidder's 10 ETH pays the 9.5 ETH debt, 0.5 ETH remainder goes to original borrower. 4) Attacker receives NFT worth 12 ETH for free.
prerequisites: Active isolate auction with ended duration
Attacker can submit transaction after auction end timestamp

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete theft of auctioned NFT collateral. Highest bidder loses escrowed funds without receiving the NFT.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None - NFT transferred to attacker, funds consumed

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying that only the highest bidder can claim the NFT after auction

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Only extraBorrowAmounts if bid < current debt, potentially zero
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain but not preventable by bidder after the fact

threats.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Missing Reentrancy Guard Enables State Manipulation During Callback
threats.hypothesis_description: flashLoanERC20 in FlashLoan.sol explicitly lacks the nonReentrant modifier (comment: 'Don't use nonReentrant modifier here'). During the callback to the receiver, the global reentrancyLock is UNLOCKED, permitting the callback to invoke any other protocol function (deposit, borrow, withdraw, liquidate). Additionally, erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan does NOT decrement assetData.availableLiquidity, so operations during callback see inflated available liquidity, enabling interest rate manipulation and incorrect validation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The reentrancy lock is the protocol's primary protection against cross-function state manipulation. Intentionally leaving it unlocked during an external callback that the attacker controls creates a wide attack surface. Combined with inflated availableLiquidity, this enables utilization rate manipulation affecting borrow/supply rates for all users.

proof_of_signal.pattern: flashLoanERC20 has whenNotPaused but NO nonReentrant. Comment explicitly states 'Don't use nonReentrant modifier here'. VaultLogic.erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan does not update availableLiquidity.

preconditions: Flash loan enabled for target asset (assetData.isFlashLoanEnabled)
Sufficient token balance in pool
Attacker deploys receiver contract implementing IFlashLoanReceiver

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No reentrancy guard + no availableLiquidity update during flash loan
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: During callback, any protocol function can be called. updateInterestRates will compute utilization with inflated availableLiquidity, setting artificially low rates.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No nonReentrant modifier, no availableLiquidity adjustment for flash-loaned tokens

analysis_questions: Can an attacker deposit flash-loaned tokens back to trigger updateInterestRates with inflated liquidity?
What is the maximum rate manipulation achievable?
Can this be chained with borrow to lock in artificially low rates?
validation_requirements: Build PoC: flash loan, deposit in callback, verify rate reduction
Quantify rate manipulation magnitude for realistic pool sizes
Verify that borrow during callback uses manipulated rates

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Flash loan large amount of Asset X. 2) In callback, deposit tokens back (updates rates with inflated liquidity -> low utilization -> low rates). 3) Borrow at manipulated low rate. 4) Withdraw deposited tokens. 5) Return flash loan. Attacker has locked in below-market borrow rate.
prerequisites: Flash loan enabled for the asset

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Interest rate manipulation affecting all lenders (reduced yield) and enabling below-market borrowing
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All lenders of the affected asset
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Rates recalculated on next operation, but damage to lenders in that block is permanent

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for reentrancy during flash loan callback or rate manipulation

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Multiple internal calls during callback ~500K gas
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Flash loan is free (no fee in code)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Rate changes visible on-chain but may appear as normal fluctuation

threats.title: Isolate Redeem Decreases Wrong User's Borrow Balance (params.msgSender Instead of Actual Borrower)
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeIsolateRedeem, VaultLogic.erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow is called with params.msgSender as the account, but the actual borrower is the NFT owner (tokenData.owner). If a third party with their own isolate borrows in the same group calls redeem on another user's auctioned loan, the caller's borrow balance is reduced instead of the actual borrower's, corrupting per-user accounting while loanData.scaledAmount is correctly reduced.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates a permanent inconsistency between the loan-level tracking (loanData.scaledAmount) and the per-user aggregate tracking (userScaledIsolateBorrow). The actual borrower cannot fully repay their other loans because their tracked borrow is higher than reality. The caller gets free debt reduction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow(debtGroupData, params.msgSender, vars.amountScaled) uses params.msgSender; should use tokenData.owner or the original borrower

preconditions: An isolate loan is in AUCTION status
Attacker has an existing isolate borrow in the same debt group with sufficient scaled balance
Auction has not ended (block.timestamp <= auctionEndTimestamp)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Wrong address parameter in borrow decrease: params.msgSender instead of actual borrower
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Caller's userScaledIsolateBorrow is decreased instead of the actual borrower's. Loan's scaledAmount correctly decreased. Accounting permanently inconsistent.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that params.msgSender == actual borrower; no derivation of borrower from tokenData

analysis_questions: Can the attacker precisely control the amount of borrow reduction they receive?
Does the accounting inconsistency prevent the actual borrower from repaying?
What is the maximum exploitable value per redeem call?
validation_requirements: Build PoC: two users with isolate borrows in same group, one in auction, other calls redeem
Verify caller's borrow decreased and actual borrower's borrow unchanged
Check if actual borrower can still interact with their other loans

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) User A has isolate loan in auction, NFT in group 1. 2) Attacker has own isolate borrow of 50 ETH in group 1. 3) Attacker calls isolateRedeem for User A's loan. 4) Attacker pays redeemAmount + bidFine. 5) Attacker's borrow reduced by redeemAmount scaled. 6) User A's borrow unchanged. 7) Net: attacker paid redeemAmount but got their own debt reduced by same amount. The bid fine is the cost.
prerequisites: Attacker has isolate borrow in same group as target loan
Target loan in auction within redeem window

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Attacker gets debt reduction at cost of only bid fine. Actual borrower's accounting corrupted.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: The actual borrower whose accounting is corrupted, and lenders who bear the bad debt
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires admin intervention to fix accounting; no automated recovery

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying that only the actual borrower's debt is reduced in redeem

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Accounting inconsistency visible in state but not in normal operations until borrower tries to repay

threats.title: Native Token Deposit/Repay/Bid Flows Broken Due to WETH Approval Requirement After Wrap
threats.hypothesis_description: wrapNativeTokenInWallet wraps ETH to WETH and transfers it to the user via IWETH.transferFrom(pool, user). Subsequently, executeDepositERC20/executeRepay/executeAuction calls erc20TransferInLiquidity which does safeTransferFrom(user, pool, amount), requiring the user to have WETH approval for the pool. Users depositing native ETH will not have pre-approved the pool for WETH, causing all native token deposit, repay, and bid transactions to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: The wrap-then-pull pattern creates a circular dependency: ETH is wrapped to WETH and sent to the user, then immediately pulled back. The pull requires WETH approval that a native ETH user wouldn't have. This breaks a core protocol feature across multiple entry points.

proof_of_signal.pattern: wrapNativeTokenInWallet transfers WETH to user via transferFrom(pool, user). Then erc20TransferInLiquidity in the caller does safeTransferFrom(user, pool, amount) requiring user WETH approval.

preconditions: User calls any native-token function (depositERC20, repay, auction, etc.) with NATIVE_TOKEN_ADDRESS
User has NOT pre-approved pool contract for WETH spending

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: WETH transferFrom(pool, user) followed by safeTransferFrom(user, pool) without user approval
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: In WETH9, transferFrom checks allowance when src != msg.sender. The pool calling safeTransferFrom(user, pool, amount) requires user's WETH allowance for the pool.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No self-approval mechanism, no use of WETH.transfer() instead of transferFrom()

analysis_questions: Does the specific WETH implementation on target chains skip allowance for self-transfers?
Are there any pre-deployment WETH approval steps in the deployment scripts?
Can users work around this by pre-approving WETH?
validation_requirements: Test native ETH deposit on target WETH implementation
Verify revert occurs at safeTransferFrom step
Check all native token code paths affected

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not an attack per se but a complete DoS of native token functionality. Any user attempting to deposit ETH, repay with ETH, or bid with ETH will have their transaction revert.
prerequisites: User interacts with NATIVE_TOKEN_ADDRESS without prior WETH approval

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete inability to use native ETH for deposits, repayments, bids, and liquidations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users attempting native ETH operations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Users can manually wrap and approve WETH as workaround

threats.test_coverage_gap: No integration test for native ETH deposit/repay flows with actual WETH contract

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Immediately detectable on first native token operation

threats.title: Native Token Withdrawal Uses Uncapped Amount for Unwrap, Causing Revert or Incorrect Transfer
threats.hypothesis_description: In BVault.withdrawERC20 for native tokens, executeWithdrawERC20 may cap params.amount to the user's actual balance (if requested amount > balance). However, the original uncapped amount variable is then passed to unwrapNativeTokenInWallet, which attempts to transferFrom(user, pool, originalAmount). This causes a revert when the original requested amount exceeds the actually withdrawn amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: The InputTypes struct is passed by memory to executeWithdrawERC20, which modifies params.amount internally. But BVault.withdrawERC20 uses the local 'amount' variable which was set before the call. When users request type(uint256).max to withdraw all, the unwrap will always fail.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdrawERC20 passes 'amount' to executeWithdrawERC20 which may cap it, then uses original 'amount' for unwrapNativeTokenInWallet

preconditions: User has native token (WETH) supply position
User requests withdrawal with amount > actual balance (e.g., type(uint256).max)
Asset is configured as NATIVE_TOKEN_ADDRESS

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: executeWithdrawERC20 modifies params.amount internally but BVault uses the pre-call amount for unwrap
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: unwrapNativeTokenInWallet(asset, msgSender, amount) uses original uncapped amount. If executeWithdrawERC20 capped to userBalance, unwrap tries to pull more WETH than was sent to receiver.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No return value from executeWithdrawERC20 reflecting actual withdrawn amount. No re-read of amount after execution.

analysis_questions: Does the struct modification propagate back to the caller?
What happens if receiver != msgSender in the native token withdrawal path?
Can this be exploited beyond DoS (e.g., pulling extra WETH from user)?
validation_requirements: Test withdrawERC20 with native token and amount > userBalance
Verify revert occurs at unwrapNativeTokenInWallet
Check if receiver parameter creates additional inconsistency

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User with 5 WETH supply calls withdrawERC20(native, type(uint256).max). executeWithdrawERC20 caps to 5 WETH and sends to receiver. unwrapNativeTokenInWallet tries to pull type(uint256).max WETH from msgSender - reverts. 'Withdraw all' for native token is permanently broken.
prerequisites: User has native token supply position

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users cannot withdraw all native token supply in a single transaction
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with native token deposits attempting max withdrawal
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Users can specify exact amount as workaround

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for native token withdrawal with amount > balance

threats.title: ERC721 Flash Loan Missing lockerAddr Check Allows Flash-Loaning Locked NFTs
threats.hypothesis_description: executeFlashLoanERC721 validates tokenData.owner == msgSender and checks isolate loan status, but does NOT check tokenData.lockerAddr. NFTs locked by yield managers (lockerAddr != address(0)) can be flash-loaned out of the pool, breaking the locker's guarantee that the NFT remains in the pool during yield operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: The lockerAddr mechanism exists to prevent NFT movement during yield staking operations. Flash loaning a locked NFT could allow the owner to: claim airdrops that should accrue to the yield strategy, manipulate the NFT state in external contracts, or create inconsistent state with the yield manager.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Checks tokenData.owner == inputParams.msgSender and loanData.loanStatus, but no check for tokenData.lockerAddr != address(0)

preconditions: NFT is deposited in isolate mode with an active locker (tokenData.lockerAddr != address(0))
NFT owner calls flashLoanERC721
Flash loan is enabled for the NFT asset

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing require(tokenData.lockerAddr == address(0)) before allowing flash loan
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: lockerAddr is checked in validateWithdrawERC721 and validateIsolateBorrowBasic but NOT in flash loan validation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No lockerAddr check in flash loan path

analysis_questions: What operations can be performed on the NFT during the flash loan callback?
Can this break yield manager accounting or allow airdrop theft?
Does the yield manager have any fallback protection?
validation_requirements: Verify that locked NFT can be flash-loaned
Test interaction with yield manager during flash loan callback
Check if NFT state manipulation during callback affects yield operations

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) User deposits NFT in isolate mode. 2) Yield manager locks NFT (lockerAddr set). 3) User calls flashLoanERC721. 4) In callback, user claims airdrop/delegate rights. 5) NFT returned. Yield manager's expectations violated.
prerequisites: Flash loan enabled for NFT asset

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Theft of airdrops, delegation rights, or other NFT-associated value that should accrue to yield strategy
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Depends on what was extracted during callback

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for flash loan on locked NFTs

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Flash loan event visible but airdrop claim may be missed

threats.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Bypasses availableLiquidity Tracking and Can Access Bid Escrow Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan performs raw safeTransfer without checking assetData.availableLiquidity. The flash loan amount is not validated against available liquidity - it can access the entire token balance including bid escrow amounts (totalBidAmout). This can temporarily disrupt auction bid refunds during the callback and violates the solvency invariant that bid amounts are always available for refund.
threats.investigation_rationale: The pool maintains separate accounting for availableLiquidity and totalBidAmout. Bid escrow must always be available for refund to outbid bidders. Flash loans bypassing this accounting can temporarily make bid refunds impossible during callback, enabling griefing of auction participants.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan does IERC20.safeTransfer without checking availableLiquidity or totalBidAmout; no validation that amount <= availableLiquidity

preconditions: Flash loan enabled for the asset
Pool holds tokens from both liquidity and bid escrow
Flash loan amount > availableLiquidity but <= total balance

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Can flash loan bid escrow funds. During callback, erc20TransferOutBidAmount would fail for auction refunds.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(amount <= assetData.availableLiquidity) before flash loan transfer

analysis_questions: Can this be combined with reentrancy to disrupt ongoing auctions?
What operations during callback rely on actual token balance vs tracked liquidity?
Can the attacker profit from temporarily disrupting bid refunds?
validation_requirements: Flash loan amount exceeding availableLiquidity but within total balance
Verify bid refund failure during callback
Test auction bidding during flash loan callback

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Pool has 100 WETH liquidity + 50 WETH bid escrow = 150 WETH balance. 2) Attacker flash loans 140 WETH (exceeds availableLiquidity but pool has balance). 3) During callback, any bid operation requiring outbid refund fails. 4) Tokens returned after callback.
prerequisites: Active auction bids in escrow for same token

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Temporary disruption of auction refunds; potential griefing of auction participants

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for flash loan accessing bid escrow amounts

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Flash loan amount visible in events

threats.title: collectFeeToTreasury Reads and Zeroes accruedFee Before Index Update, Missing Newly Accrued Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeCollectFeeToTreasury, accruedFee is read and set to zero BEFORE calling updateInterestIndexs. The index update calls _accrueFeeToTreasury which adds new fees to assetData.accruedFee. Since accruedFee was already zeroed, only previously accrued fees are collected; fees generated between the last index update and the current block are deferred to the next collection. While not lost, this systematically under-collects fees per call.
threats.investigation_rationale: The ordering of operations causes the treasury to always miss one update period's worth of fees per collection. Over many collections, this creates a growing delay in fee realization, reducing treasury income timing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: accruedFee read and zeroed at line ~45-47, then updateInterestIndexs called at line ~49 which internally calls _accrueFeeToTreasury adding new fees to the now-zeroed accruedFee

preconditions: Caller is pool admin
Asset has non-zero accruedFee
Time has passed since lastUpdateTimestamp (new fees to accrue)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: accruedFee = 0 before updateInterestIndexs which accrues new fees
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Correct order should be: updateInterestIndexs first, then read and zero accruedFee
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No re-read of accruedFee after index update

analysis_questions: What is the magnitude of fees missed per collection?
Can this be exploited strategically (e.g., admin calls collect right after a large borrow)?
Are deferred fees ever permanently lost?
validation_requirements: Calculate fee difference between correct and incorrect ordering
Verify fees are preserved in accruedFee for next collection
Quantify financial impact over protocol lifetime

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not directly exploitable for profit, but systematic under-collection of treasury fees. Each collection misses fees accrued since last index update until current block.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Delayed fee collection reducing treasury income timing; fees not lost but deferred
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Fees collected on next call; no permanent loss

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying exact fee collection amount with fresh accrual

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Subtle - collected amount slightly less than expected

threats.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Missing Balance Delta Verification Enables Token Drain with Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan and erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan perform raw safeTransfer/safeTransferFrom without pre/post balance checks. Unlike erc20TransferInLiquidity and erc20TransferOutLiquidity which verify balance deltas, flash loan transfers do not validate that the exact amount was received. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the pool loses value on each flash loan (fee deducted on both outgoing and incoming transfer).
threats.investigation_rationale: The project documentation explicitly states support for fee-on-transfer tokens. Other transfer functions in VaultLogic have balance delta checks, but flash loan transfers do not. This creates a slow drain vector through repeated flash loans.
deployment_assumptions: A fee-on-transfer ERC20 token is configured as a pool asset with flash loan enabled
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation states 'ERC20 (Any implementation, explicitly including weird traits: fee-on-transfer)'. No deployment evidence of specific fee-on-transfer tokens configured.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan and erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan lack pre/post balance checks present in erc20TransferInLiquidity (lines 300-310)

preconditions: A fee-on-transfer token is configured as pool asset
Flash loan is enabled for that token
Token charges fee on both transfer directions

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No balanceOf checks before/after transfers unlike other transfer functions
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: For 1% fee-on-transfer: flash loan 1000, receiver gets 990, returns 1000 but pool receives 990. Pool loses 10 per cycle.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing pre/post balance checks, missing require(poolSizeAfter >= poolSizeBefore)

analysis_questions: Which configured tokens have fee-on-transfer behavior?
How many flash loan cycles to drain significant liquidity?
Does the pool's tracked availableLiquidity become inconsistent with actual balance?
validation_requirements: Test with fee-on-transfer token mock
Verify pool balance decreases after flash loan round-trip
Calculate drain rate for realistic fee percentages

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Pool has 10000 FEE_TOKEN (1% transfer fee). 2) Attacker flash loans 10000. Pool sends 10000, attacker receives 9900. 3) Attacker approves and returns 10000. Pool receives 9900. 4) Pool lost 200 tokens total (2 * 1% * 10000). 5) Repeat.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Gradual drain of fee-on-transfer token liquidity through repeated flash loans
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors of the affected token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires disabling flash loan for affected tokens

threats.test_coverage_gap: No flash loan test with fee-on-transfer tokens

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Can be repeated rapidly in multiple transactions
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Pool balance decreasing without corresponding state changes

threats.title: Cross Liquidation ERC721 Integer Division Truncation in Per-Item Debt Calculation
threats.hypothesis_description: In _calculateDebtAmountFromERC721Collateral, collateralItemDebtToCover = collateralTotalDebtToCover / userCollateralBalance performs integer division. When the borrower has many NFTs, the per-item debt is rounded down. For N tokens being liquidated, the total debt covered is N * floor(totalDebt/totalNFTs), which is less than the proportional debt. The liquidator pays less than they should for the NFTs, and the borrower's remaining debt is higher than expected.
threats.investigation_rationale: This systematic rounding benefits liquidators at the expense of borrowers. With many NFTs and debt that doesn't divide evenly, the rounding error could be significant enough to create an unfair liquidation outcome.

proof_of_signal.pattern: collateralItemDebtToCover = collateralTotalDebtToCover / liqVars.userCollateralBalance - integer division truncation

preconditions: Borrower has multiple ERC721 collateral tokens
Debt amount doesn't divide evenly by number of NFTs
Health factor below liquidation threshold

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Integer division of debt by NFT count rounds down per-item, then multiplied by liquidated count
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: For 100 NFTs with 999 debt, per-item = 9 (not 9.99). Liquidating 50 NFTs covers 450 instead of 499.5
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No rounding up, no minimum debt coverage enforcement

analysis_questions: What is the maximum rounding loss in realistic scenarios?
Can this be exploited to liquidate NFTs while covering minimal debt?
Does the remainDebtToLiquidate mechanism compensate?
validation_requirements: Calculate maximum rounding error for realistic NFT counts and debt amounts
Verify impact on borrower's remaining position
Check if the surplus mechanism (remainDebtToLiquidate) covers the gap

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Liquidator selects borrower with many NFTs where debt/NFTcount has large remainder. Per-item debt is rounded down significantly. Liquidator covers less debt per NFT than proportional share, getting NFTs at a discount beyond the intended liquidation bonus.
prerequisites: Borrower position underwater with multiple ERC721 collateral

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Systematic under-payment by liquidators, excess bad debt left to borrower/protocol
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Borrowers being liquidated with many NFTs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Individual losses may be small but accumulate across many liquidations

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for ERC721 liquidation with non-divisible debt amounts

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible in debt coverage calculations but requires careful analysis

threats.title: Isolate Liquidation Passes Inflated liquidityAdded to updateInterestRates
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeIsolateLiquidate, updateInterestRates is called with liquidityAdded = (vars.totalBorrowAmount + vars.totalExtraAmount). However, totalBorrowAmount is repaid from bid escrow (erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity moves from totalBidAmout to availableLiquidity), and only totalExtraAmount is new external liquidity. The interest rate calculation sees inflated liquidity addition, computing lower utilization and rates than warranted.
threats.investigation_rationale: The rate calculation formula is: availableLiquidityPlusDebt = availableLiquidity + liquidityAdded - liquidityTaken + totalDebt. If liquidityAdded is double-counted (bid escrow already in pool balance + counted as new liquidity), utilization drops artificially, setting lower rates for all borrowers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: updateInterestRates(poolData, debtAssetData, (vars.totalBorrowAmount + vars.totalExtraAmount), 0). But erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity already increased availableLiquidity by totalBorrowAmount.

preconditions: Isolate auction has ended
Liquidation is executed
Significant bid amounts relative to pool size

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: liquidityAdded includes totalBorrowAmount from bid escrow which already increased availableLiquidity via erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Double-counting: bid escrow moves to availableLiquidity (increasing it), then updateInterestRates adds it again as liquidityAdded
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should pass only totalExtraAmount as liquidityAdded, not totalBorrowAmount + totalExtraAmount

analysis_questions: How significant is the rate impact for typical liquidation sizes?
Does this create persistent rate miscalculation or just transient?
Can an attacker strategically trigger liquidations to manipulate rates?
validation_requirements: Trace the exact liquidity flow during isolate liquidation
Calculate rate difference with correct vs inflated liquidityAdded
Verify if availableLiquidity + liquidityAdded is double-counted

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Large isolate liquidation with 100 ETH bid amount. erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity increases availableLiquidity by 100. updateInterestRates gets liquidityAdded=100+extra, seeing 200 ETH of new liquidity instead of just extra. Utilization drops, rates decrease artificially.
prerequisites: Isolate auction ending with significant bid amounts

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Artificially suppressed interest rates after liquidations, reducing lender yield
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All lenders of the affected asset
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Rates corrected on next operation, but damage in that block is permanent

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying correct interest rate update after isolate liquidation

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Rate changes visible but may appear normal
